content: The most pervasive failure mode in embedded systems using the embassy executor is treating cooperative multitasking as if it were a preemptive operating system, which leads to silent data corruption and system hangs that are incredibly difficult to debug. When you implement a real-world use case like an LED chaser with a temperature sensor reading on an ESP32, the naive developer immediately writes code expecting tasks to yield control automatically or to be interrupted at arbitrary points by higher priority work. They assume #[embassy_executor::task] will magically serialize access to shared hardware resources just like threads in Linux do, but it does not; the executor is merely a scheduler that runs your coroutines sequentially until each one explicitly yields via the Send trait bound on its channel types. The fundamental misconception here is believing that async code inherently prevents race conditions without explicit synchronization primitives; in reality, if two tasks try to write to the same GPIO pin or update the same temperature register without locking, the first write will be completely lost when the second task overwrites it, and because there is no OS kernel to detect this violation, your system simply behaves incorrectly until you manually insert a Mutex<T> around every shared resource access. Another subtle pitfall involves misunderstanding how embassy handles interrupts; many developers assume that an interrupt service routine can freely block the executor or perform heavy computation without consequence, but the embassy model dictates that interrupt handlers must be lightweight and non-blocking to avoid starving the main task loop, meaning you cannot simply call #[embassy_executor::task] from within an ISR expecting it to spawn a new background job. The documentation often glosses over the fact that your application logic must explicitly manage state transitions between tasks using channels like Channel<u8> or Arc<Mutex<State>> rather than relying on implicit sharing, and failing to understand this leads to deadlocks where one task waits for a sender that never receives because another task is holding the lock indefinitely. Furthermore, there is a dangerous tendency to forget that the embassy executor runs in a single thread on many microcontrollers, so you cannot parallelize work across cores without careful coordination using core-local buffers or inter-core messaging via the embassy intrinsics crate; assuming multi-core parallelism by default results in corrupted shared memory when multiple tasks try to access overlapping registers simultaneously. The correct mental model requires viewing your embedded application as a single-threaded program where async code is merely a way to express non-blocking I/O patterns that you must manually orchestrate, meaning every peripheral driver like an SPI flash or UART interface should be wrapped in its own dedicated task that yields frequently via the yield!() function whenever it completes an operation to allow other critical tasks to run. You must also recognize that error handling in this environment is strictly manual; there is no automatic panic recovery or exception propagation across threads, so you must explicitly propagate Result<T> values through your channel types and ensure every task path returns a valid outcome without unwinding the stack unexpectedly. Finally, the most common mistake involves ignoring the startup timing constraints of the executor, assuming that spawning tasks happens instantaneously at boot time, when in fact the initialization sequence for the embassy runtime can introduce latency if not carefully sequenced with your hardware clock configuration, so you must pre-calculate and reserve enough flash space for all your task metadata before attempting to initialize the system.
